coredata.dc:title: Application of graphene quantum dots for simultaneous fluorescence imaging and tumor-targeted drug delivery
coredata.dc:creator: Dong, Jian
Wang, Kaiqi
Sun, Liping
Sun, Baoliang
Yang, Mingfeng
Chen, Hongyu
Wang, Yi
Sun, Jingyi
Dong, Lifeng
coredata.dc:description: A novel drug delivery system based on arginine-glycine-aspartic acid (RGD)-conjugated graphene quantum dots (GQDs) was synthesized and utilized to load the antitumor drug doxorubicin (DOX) for targeted cancer fluorescence imaging as well as tracking and monitoring drug delivery without the need for external dyes. The inherent stable fluorescence of GQDs enables real-time monitoring of the cellular uptake of the DOX-GQDs-RGD nano-assembly and the consequent release of DOX. The release of DOX demonstrated strong pH dependence and implies hydrogen-bonding interaction between GQDs and DOX, an observation that suggests the possibility for GQDs serving as pH-sensitive drug carriers. As a nanocarrier, GQDs unlinked with DOX were nontoxic to U251 human glioma cells. Compared to free DOX, DOX-GQDs-RGD conjugates demonstrated substantial cytotoxicity to U251 glioma cells within a broad range of DOX concentrations. After applying the GQDs as drug carriers, the drug efficacy of DOX improved without concomitant DOX dosage increases. Cellular uptake results of DOX-GQDs-RGD conjugates indicate that not only DOX but also some GQDs penetrated into cell nuclei after 16h of incubation. This enhancement combined with efficient nuclear delivery improved the cytotoxicity of DOX dramatically. This type of drug delivery system based on GQDs may find widespread applications in biomedicine.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Drug delivery
Fluorescence imaging
Doxorubicin
RGD

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Observation of DOX release from GQDs in cells

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of GQDs and its HRTEM image (inset). (b) Fourier transform infrared spectrum of GQDs.

(a) PL spectra of GQDs under different excitation wavelengths of 340–520nm. The inset is the optical image of GQDs solution under a UV beam of 365nm. (b) PL intensity of GQDs solution with different pH values.
UV–vis absorption spectra of GQDs, DOX and DOX-GQDs-RGD. (b) PL spectra of DOX and DOX-GQDs-RGD at 480 excitation wavelength. (c) The release of DOX from DOX-GQDs-RGD at different pH values.
(a) Viability studies of GQDs performed in Glioma cancer cell line at different concentrations. (b) Viability studies of GQDs, free DOX, GQDs-DOX, and DOX-GQDs-RGD at different DOX concentrations.

CLSM images of U251 Glioma cells treated with DOX-GQDs-RGD after incubation for 2, 8 and 16h excited by a 405-nm laser: (a) GQD signals collected in the range of 525±50nm, (b) DOX signals collected in the range of 595±50nm, (c) merged images of GQDs and DOX, (d) cell nuclei stained with DAPI and signals collected from 450±50nm, and (e) merged images of GQDs, DOX and DAPI.

head.ce:title: Application of graphene quantum dots for simultaneous fluorescence imaging and tumor-targeted drug delivery

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Engineering, Taishan Medical University, Taian, Shandong 271016, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Cerebral Microcirculation in Universities of Shandong and Department of Neurology of Affiliated Hospital, Taishan Medical University, Taian, Shandong 271016, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Cerebral Microcirculation in Universities of Shandong and Department of Neurology of Affiliated Hospital

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Optometry and Department of Ophthalmology of Affiliated Hospital, Taishan Medical University, Taian, Shandong 271016, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Optometry and Department of Ophthalmology of Affiliated Hospital

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Wonju Severance Christian Hospital, Yonsei University Wonju College of Medicine, Wonju, Gangwon, 220-701, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Yonsei University Wonju College of Medicine

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel drug delivery system based on arginine-glycine-aspartic acid (RGD)-conjugated graphene quantum dots (GQDs) was synthesized and utilized to load the antitumor drug doxorubicin (DOX) for targeted cancer fluorescence imaging as well as tracking and monitoring drug delivery without the need for external dyes. The inherent stable fluorescence of GQDs enables real-time monitoring of the cellular uptake of the DOX-GQDs-RGD nano-assembly and the consequent release of DOX. The release of DOX demonstrated strong pH dependence and implies hydrogen-bonding interaction between GQDs and DOX, an observation that suggests the possibility for GQDs serving as pH-sensitive drug carriers. As a nanocarrier, GQDs unlinked with DOX were nontoxic to U251 human glioma cells. Compared to free DOX, DOX-GQDs-RGD conjugates demonstrated substantial cytotoxicity to U251 glioma cells within a broad range of DOX concentrations. After applying the GQDs as drug carriers, the drug efficacy of DOX improved without concomitant DOX dosage increases. Cellular uptake results of DOX-GQDs-RGD conjugates indicate that not only DOX but also some GQDs penetrated into cell nuclei after 16h of incubation. This enhancement combined with efficient nuclear delivery improved the cytotoxicity of DOX dramatically. This type of drug delivery system based on GQDs may find widespread applications in biomedicine.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer as a leading cause of human death has attracted substantial research interest in the past several decades. To date, chemotherapy is one of the major treatment options for cancer. However, conventional chemotherapy using solo anticancer drugs suffers from several limitations, such as low water solubility, poor bioavailability, nonspecific selectivity, low local therapeutic concentration as well as adverse side effects to normal cells [1]. For instance, doxorubicin (DOX) is among the most effective anthracycline antibiotics for tumor therapeutics. However, its clinical usage is always accompanied by severe side effects such as cardiotoxicity and intrinsic or acquired tumor drug resistance. Therefore, development of novel DOX delivery systems to abrogate drug resistance and minimize side effects, thus improving the therapeutic efficacy of DOX, would be both desirable and necessary [2].

In the past decade, various graphene based nanomaterials, such as nano-graphene oxide (nGO) [1,3–6], have been developed as carriers for cancer chemotherapeutic drug delivery by enhancing water solubility and bioavailability to improve treatment efficacy and reduce systemic toxicity [3]. However, nGO demonstrates a very low fluorescence efficiency, a characteristic which limits the tracking of this material within complex living organisms and even within individual cells [7]. Therefore, drug delivery systems based on nGO should be visualized using organic dyes [8] or semiconductor quantum dots [9] in order to understand cellular uptake processes. However, due to the fluorescence quenching nature of nGO [8] and the toxicity of organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots [10,11], the materials employed as drug delivery systems face certain limitations in practical applications. Therefore, development of a functional luminescent nano-system that can perform imaging and drug delivery tasks, without the need for external dyes, is desirable [12]. Furthermore, drug carrier particle size influences drug loading and releasing kinetics. The cellular uptake and bioavailability performance of smaller particles has been found to be better than that for larger particles [13]. Due to the relatively large size of nGO, in vivo applications might be compromised due to the high risk of blocked arteries where the carrier can end up in the circulatory system [7]. These challenges and limitations have driven the further development of nano-vehicles.

As a new type of graphene based nanomaterial, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are emerging as promising materials for biomedical applications. They are being extensively investigated for cellular imaging and drug delivery due to their high intrinsic fluorescence, high photostability, low toxicity, large surface area, in addition to being rich in delocalized π electrons with a variety of adjacent functional groups including carboxyl, hydroxyl, carbonyl, and epoxy groups. These characteristics provide enormous potential for GQDs as a drug delivery platform through π-π interactions and the functionalization of oxygen groups [14,15]. A common cancer drug used in conjunction with GQDs is DOX, which interferes with DNA replication, transcription, and damage repair by direct intercalation with DNA helices. DOX binds to surface aromatic groups of GQDs by π-π stacking and hydrophobic interactions, but the release of DOX is pH responsive. Release tends to occur under acidic conditions especially, a favorable phenomenon considering that the extracellular environment of tumor cells is more acidic compared to normal cells [15]. Miao et al. [16] reported that GQDs could efficiently deliver DOX selectively to the cell nucleus and could also enhance DNA cleavage activity of DOX markedly, thus improving its efficacy. Furthermore, the DOX/GQD conjugates could also increase the nuclear uptake into, and cytotoxicity of DOX for, drug-resistant cancer cells, indicating that the conjugates may be capable of improving chemotherapeutic efficacy of anti-cancer drugs that are otherwise suboptimal due to drug resistance.

GQDs have been shown to be easily functionalized with targeting molecules such as folic acid (FA) [12,17], arginine-glycine-aspartic acid (RGD) [7,10], hyaluronic acid (HA) [18,19], and antibodies [20], which can be used to target surface receptors and increase internalization of GQDs through endocytosis. This approach has the potential to improve drug effectiveness by increasing the amount that enters the target cells for a particular drug dose, while decreasing detrimental effects to healthy cells and the likelihood or severity of side effects caused by the drug [15]. Moreover, due to their lower toxicity than semiconductor quantum dots and high photostability compared to organic dyes [21], the intrinsic fluorescence properties of the GQDs allow the surveillance of the whole process of internalization, intracellular distribution, and release [7]. For instance, Wang et al. [12] utilized FA-conjugated GQDs to load DOX. The fabricated nano-assembly can unambiguously discriminate cancer cells from normal cells and efficiently deliver the drug to targeted cells. The inherent fluorescence stability of GQDs enables real-time monitoring of the cellular uptake of the DOX–GQD–FA nano-assembly and the consequent release of drugs. Qiu et al. [7] reported a multifunctional drug delivery system consisting of GQDs, RGD, and DOX, that permits simultaneous tracking and targeted therapy of prostate cancer cells.

Inspired by previous studies, we decided to develop a novel drug delivery system for simultaneously visualizing and targeting cancer cells, taking full advantage of the interesting optical properties of GQDs and the specific recognition capability of RGD. In this study, fluorescent GQDs were synthesized and conjugated with RGD to demonstrate highly selective and specific tumor cell imaging. The RGD-conjugated GQDs (GQDs-RGD) were then employed as a carrier of the antitumor drug DOX for targeted cell delivery. The cellular uptake of the DOX-GQDs-RGD and the consequent drug release were monitored simultaneously in two separate channels, corresponding to GQDs and DOX, in order to reveal their localization and relative internalization kinetics. We found that DOX-GQDs-RGD complexes exhibit a significantly higher lethal effect compared with both GQDs-DOX and free DOX. However, naked GQDs under the same conditions display almost no toxicity for U251 human glioma cells. These results mean that DOX-GQDs-RGD could improve the drug efficacy of DOX without increasing the DOX dose. These results also imply that GQDs are a potentially ideal nanomaterial for improving the efficacy of anti-cancer drugs.

Graphene oxide (GO) was purchased from Nanjing XFNANO Materials Tech Co. (China). Concentrated nitric acid was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. (China). The following reagents were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (America): 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) and RGD. DOX, Roswell Park Memorial Institute medium (RPMI), penicillin-streptomycin, 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI), dialysis bag (MWCO 3500Da), and 4% paraformaldehyde were obtained from Solarbio Science & Technology Co. (China). Trypsin-EDTA solution (0.25%) was obtained from Life Technologies (America). Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was purchased from Zhejiang Tianhang biotechnology Co. (China). 3-(4, 5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2, 5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) was purchased from Nanjing Infogate biochemical technology Co. (China). All solutions were prepared with 0.01M phosphate buffered saline (PBS, pH=7.4).

GQDs were obtained from thermally exfoliated graphite oxide by refluxing with concentrated nitric acid as previously described [22]. Briefly, 0.2g of GO powder was placed in a quartz tube that was sealed at one end. The other end was closed using a rubber stopper, and an argon (Ar) inlet was inserted through the stopper. The sample was flushed with Ar for 10s, and then the quartz tube was quickly inserted into a tube furnace preheated to 1050°C and held in the furnace for 30s. This was followed by refluxing the powder in HNO3 (6M) for 24h. The solution was then cooled and centrifuged at 5000rpm for 10min. The supernatant was heated at 200°C to evaporate water and HNO3, and a reddish-brown solid was obtained. The resulting powder was subsequently dispersed in deionized water and dialyzed to remove ions and other impurities.

The GQD samples were diluted for optical characterization. UV–vis spectra were recorded on a U-3900 spectrophotometer (Hitachi High Technologies). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained using an F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi High Technologies). The morphology of GQDs was characterized by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, Tecnai G2 F20). The GQDs powder was characterized using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (Avater 370, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Raman spectrum was obtained using a laser confocal micro-Raman microscope (Horiba Co., Lab HR800, Japan).

GQD PBS solution (5mL, 5mg/mL) was allowed to react with EDC (4mg) and NHS (6mg) under magnetic stirring for 30min at room temperature. RGD (5mg) was added into the solution and reacted at room temperature for 2h under magnetic stirring. Finally, the GQDs-RGD conjugate solution was repeatedly dialyzed against PBS buffer at room temperature for 48h (dialysate was replaced every 4h) to remove residual EDC and NHS molecules. After dialysis, the purified GQDs-RGD solution was refrigerated at 4°C for later use.

DOX was loaded by adding 5mL of DOX (5mg/mL) into the GQDs or GQDs-RGD solution under stirring for 24h in darkness at room temperature. The obtained solution was repeatedly dialyzed against PBS buffer at room temperature for 48h (dialysate was replaced every 4h) to remove unloaded DOX molecules. The DOX loading amount was determined by UV–vis spectra (U-3900, Hitachi, Japan) at a wavelength of 480nm. A standard DOX PBS solution was prepared for quantitative analysis. The DOX loading efficiency was calculated as follows: DOX loading efficiency (%)=(mass of DOX added − mass of free DOX)/(mass of carriers)×100%.

The DOX release behavior was studied by fluorescence spectroscopy at 590nm with the excited wavelength of 480nm. The release behavior of DOX from GQDs-RGD was monitored in buffer solution with various pH values (i.e., 5.0, 6.0 and 7.4). DOX release at pH 5.0 and 6.0 was performed as follows: 5mL of 5mg/mL DOX-GQDs-RGD used for release experiments was placed into a dialysis bag (MWCO 3500Da) and dialyzed in 50mL of an acetate buffer solution with a pH of 5.0 or 6.0. The release reservoirs were placed on a shaking bed at 37°C with a rotation speed of 150rpm, and 3mL release media in the reservoirs was taken out for analysis at desired time intervals (2, 4, 6, 8, 12, 20, 24, 26, 28 and 72h) and replenished with an equal volume of fresh media. DOX release at pH 7.4 was similar to the procedure above, except that the dialysate buffer was changed to PBS solution (pH 7.4).

The U251 human glioma cells were cultured in RPMI medium mixed with 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% penicillin and streptomycin. Cells were incubated in a breathable culture bottle under a 5% CO2 environment at 37°C. When the cells grew to confluency at the bottom of culture bottle, passage would be executed.

The in vitro cytotoxicity of DOX-GQDs-RGD, GQDs-DOX, free GQDs, and DOX towards U251 glioma cells was evaluated by the MTT cell viability assay, respectively. The cells were seeded into 96-well plates at a density of 5×103 cells in each well. After 24h of incubation at 37°C in a 5% CO2 humidified atmosphere, the DOX-GQDs-RGD, GQDs-DOX, free GQDs and DOX at different concentrations in RPMI medium were added into the culture medium and cells were further cultured for 24h. After discarding the medium, 20μL of freshly prepared MTT tetrazolium dye (5mg/mL in PBS) was added to each well and incubated for 4h. After removing the MTT medium solution, the cells were lysed by adding 100μL of DMSO. The plates were shaken for 5min to dissolve the formazan and then the absorbance of purple formazan at 490nm was monitored using a microplate reader (Infinite M200 Pro, Tecon, Switzerland). The cell viability was calculated using the following equation:
Cell viability (%)=(optical density of the cells treated with samples/optical density of the control) ×100%.
Here, control refers to the cells cultured in the culture medium with PBS treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Observation of DOX release from GQDs in cells
ce:section.ce:para: The U251 glioma cells were seeded in a culture flask and cultured at 37°C under 5% CO2. All cells were incubated until approximately 70% confluence was achieved. Subsequently, the culture medium was replaced with culture medium containing free DOX or DOX-GQDs-RGD at a DOX concentration of 2.5μg/mL. After incubation for different times, the glass coverslips were washed three times with PBS and subjected to fluorescence imaging using a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, Nikon, Japan). To avoid the influence of fluorescence cross talk between the GQDs and DOX, the GQDs were excited by a 488-nm laser and signals were collected from 475 to 575nm; DOX was excited by a 488nm laser and signals were collected from 545 to 645nm. For DAPI staining experiments, before imaging by CLSM, cells were first incubated with DAPI for 10min. The DAPI was excited by a 405-nm laser and signals were collected from 400 to 500nm.

Various methods have been used to cut graphene-based materials through controlled oxidation or reduction processes to fabricate fluorescent GQDs, which show different morphological and optical properties depending on the sizes of the final products and the chemical groups at the edge [12]. Among them, acidic exfoliation/oxidation is considered to be an efficient approach to produce GQDs in bulk quantity from abundant raw materials [23]. Moreover, these GQDs are rich in oxygen-related functional groups on their surfaces, a helpful characteristic for tuning their optical properties by surface modification and edge effects [24]. The GQDs used in this study were synthesized from thermally exfoliated graphite oxide by refluxing with concentrated nitric acid [22]. As given in Fig. 1 a, GQDs are well dispersed with a narrow size distribution. Their average diameter is 3.7nm as measured from 100 individual particles. Discernible lattice structure of GQDs indicates that resultant nanoparticles have crystal structures. Representative HRTEM image (inset of Fig. 1 and Fig. S1 in Supplementary Data) displays a lattice spacing distance of 0.21nm, which matches the lattice constant of (100). The results above suggest that the nanoparticles are composed of crystal cores of graphitic sp2 carbon atoms. Raman spectrum of GQDs (Fig. S2) exhibits typical D band (1330cm−1) and G band (1592cm−1) of graphene, which correspond to disordered defects and graphitic structure, respectively.

The Fourier transform infrared spectrum in Fig. 1b indicates that the GQDs contain many chemical groups, including OH, CO, and epoxy/ether. The presence of such a variety of functional groups can explain why the GQDs exhibit good solubility and excellent fluorescence properties. Furthermore, these functional groups clearly facilitate post-modification of the GQDs with targeting moieties. The FT-IR spectrum of GQDs-RGD is shown in Fig. S3.

Fig. 2 presents the PL spectra of GQDs. As shown in Fig. 2a, when excited at wavelengths from 340 to 460nm, GQDs exhibit only a slight excitation dependent emission with the maximum emission at 514nm, corresponding to a PL excitation peak centered at 460nm. However, when the excitation wavelength is changed from 480 to 520nm, the emission wavelength demonstrates an obvious red-shift from 529 to 566nm and the PL intensity decreases markedly. Under irradiation with a 365nm UV lamp, GQD solution color is bright yellow-green. Furthermore, the GQDs show good fluorescence stability at different pH values from 3 to 11; whereas, under strong acid (pH=1–2) or strong alkaline environments (pH=12–14), the GDQ fluorescence intensity obviously diminishes (Fig. 2b).

The antitumor drug DOX was used to evaluate the feasibility of using GQDs-RGD as a drug carrier. DOX was loaded by mixing with GQDs-RGD in PBS buffer overnight in the dark, and the unbound DOX was removed by repeated dialysis for 48h. GQDs offer abundant aromatic rings to adsorb DOX through π-π stacking and hydrophobic interactions [12]. Loading of the anticancer drug onto GQDs-RGD is demonstrated by the characteristic absorption band of DOX shown in Fig. 3 a. Significant variations in the spectrum of DOX-GQDs-RGD are observed, e.g., a distinct DOX absorption peak at around 500nm; this peak is probably due to the presence of the dihydroxyanthraquinone chromophore [17]. At same time, spectral absorption around 300nm probably originates from the GQDs. The absorption peak of DOX attached on the GQDs is bathochromic-shifted by ∼20nm compared with that of the bare DOX in solution; this shift is generally considered to be due to π-π stacking and hydrophobic interactions between DOX and GQDs-RGD. These results indicate that DOX is successfully loaded onto GQDs-RGD. The loading efficiency of DOX onto GQDs-RGD is about 54.6wt% according to the UV–vis absorbance standard curve of DOX at 480nm (Fig. S4). Fig. 3b shows the fluorescence spectra of DOX and DOX-GQDs-RGD at 480nm excitation wavelength. The fluorescence of DOX in the DOX-GQDs-RGD is considerably less intense than that of the free DOX solution. This phenomenon is likely due to the electron transfer induced fluorescence quench as a consequence of DOX attachment onto the GQDs surface. Similar phenomena were observed in π-stacked DOX onto carbon nanotubes [25] and doxorubicin hydrochloride onto GO [26]. These results further confirm that there is a strong π-π stacking interaction between GQDs-RGD and DOX [27].

The DOX release from DOX-GQDs-RGD at a temperature of 37°C in the buffer solution (pH 5.0, 6.0 and 7.4) is illustrated in Fig. 3c. The experimental parameters 37°C and pH (5.0 and 7.4) were selected because of the similarity to physiological conditions. Cell cytosol displays a pH value of around 7.4, whereas the endocytic vesicles, wherein particles are entrapped upon internalization, have a lower pH that can achieve values of 4.5–5.5 [7]. Quantification of DOX release from the GQDs was performed under acidic conditions (pH 5.0) and alkaline conditions (pH 7.4), mimicking the environment of intracellular vesicles or of the cytosol, respectively. The release behavior of DOX from DOX-GQDs-RGD at pH of 5.0 and 7.4 during 72h is shown in Fig. 3c, which indicates a same trend of continuous release with time, but with different amounts of DOX depending on the pH. At near-neutral pH (7.4), the DOX releases slowly from DOX-GQDs-RGD, and the release rate gradually declines after 12h. Under acidic conditions (pH 5.0) on the other hand, the release rate of DOX was significantly higher than that under near-neutral conditions. The total amounts of DOX released after 72h are 40.1%, 19.5% and 9.2% at pH of 5.0, 6.0 and 7.4, respectively. The pH-dependent releasing may be due to the different degree of hydrogen-bonding interaction between these two species under different pH conditions. Under near-neutral conditions, four kinds of hydrogen bonding can be formed: between COOH of GQDs and the −OH of DOX; −COOH of GQDs and the −NH2 of DOX; −OH of GQDs and the −OH of DOX; and −OH of GQDs and the −NH2 of DOX. Under acidic conditions, −NH2 of DOX forms −NH3 + with H+ and therefore cannot participate in hydrogen bonding. In this case, two kinds of hydrogen bonding can occur: between −COOH of GQDs and the −OH of DOX; and between −OH of GQDs and the −OH of DOX. Furthermore, the H+ in solution would compete with the hydrogen-bond-forming groups and then weaken the above hydrogen-bonding interactions. Therefore, hydrogen-bonding interactions between DOX and GQDs are more prominent under near-neutral conditions, resulting in a controlled release [27,28]. Under acidic conditions, the −NH2 of DOX becomes protonated, resulting in the partial dissociation of hydrogen-bonding interaction; hence, the amount of DOX released from DOX-GQDs-RGD is more substantial. In addition, this phenomenon may also attribute to much higher hydrophilicity and solubility of DOX under acidic conditions than those under near-neutral conditions [7,29], which allows the release of a large amount of DOX from DOX-GQDs-RGD. Regarding the interaction between the GQDs and DOX, π-π stacking may be the most important factor because the loading of DOX on GQDs remains high in spite of decreasing hydrogen-bonding interactions under acidic conditions. The obtained results are in close agreement with an earlier report concerning DOX and GO [27]. This pH-influenced property of DOX-GQDs-RGD is highly desirable and useful for targeted cancer therapy, because the intracellular pH value of many tumor cells is one order of magnitude lower than that of normal tissues, and tumor cells normally exhibit an acidic intracellular environment, which is unlike their near-neutral extracellular environment [17]. Thus, this pH sensitive DOX-release behavior of DOX-GQDs-RGD should be favorable for the targeting of cancer cells while exerting minimal influence on normal cells. This result also suggests the possibility of GQDs serving as pH-sensitive drug carriers.

GQDs should demonstrate good biocompatibility for being practically employed in bioimaging and drug delivery applications. The toxicity of as-synthesized GQDs was evaluated using U251 human glioma cells by MTT assays. As seen in Fig. 4 (a), no significant change in the viability of glioma cells was found at GQD concentrations of up to 100μg/mL after incubation for 24h. When the concentration of GQDs increased to 200μg/mL, more than 90% of the glioma cells survived. The concentration used in practical bioimaging experimental testing is about 2.5μg/mL, which is much lower than this value. Therefore, positive biocompatibility associated with their favorable optical properties makes GQDs ideal candidates for particle tracking. The in vitro cytotoxicity of DOX-GQDs-RGD to glioma cells in comparison with DOX, GQDs, and GQDs-DOX was determined by a growth inhibition assay in order to evaluate the efficiency of targeted delivery of DOX via DOX-GQDs-RGD. As shown in Fig. 4(b), DOX alone exhibits cytotoxicity for glioma cells. The bare GQDs under the same conditions demonstrate almost no toxicity to glioma cells. However, both GQDs-DOX and DOX-GQDs-RGD exhibit a significantly higher lethal effect compared with free DOX. After applying the GQDs as a drug carrier, cytotoxicity of DOX to glioma cells was enhanced, an observation that is consistent with a report in the literature [30]. Improved cell killing in the presence of GQDs is consistent with the observation for DOX derivatives, in which elevated DNA cleavage activity corresponds to an increased cytotoxic potency [25,26]. Moreover, in comparison with GQDs-DOX, the DOX-GQDs-RGD assembly exhibits significant cytotoxicity for glioma cells. This elevated cytotoxicity could be attributed to the enhanced cellular uptake of DOX-GQDs-RGD assisted by the RGD sequence. This result indirectly confirms the value of RGD for targeting cancer cells, facilitating uptake of the conjugate and increasing the availability of DOX to the cells [7]. Furthermore, it is noteworthy that DOX-GQDs-RGD bearing a low DOX concentration onboard exhibited substantial cytotoxicity similar to free DOX at high concentration. For instance, the viability of the U251 glioma cells was 70.9% for free DOX with a concentration of 5μg/mL. When the concentration of DOX-GQDs-RGD is 1μg/mL, similar cell inhibition effect can be achieved and cell viability is 74.8%. At a concentration of 1.5μg/mL, the cell inhibition effect was far greater than at a free DOX concentration of 5μg/mL, and the cell viability was 60.8%. These results clearly indicate that a GQDs-RGD nanocarrier could be used to load a low DOX concentration for achieving similar treatment success obtained by using a higher concentration of free DOX, i.e., a GQDs-RGD nanocarrier could improve the drug efficacy of DOX without increasing the DOX dosage.

As a drug carrier, GQDs demonstrate good biocompatibility and excellent fluorescent properties. With the use of fluorescent GQDs, the uptake of the DOX-GQDs-RGD conjugate could be simultaneously monitored through two channels corresponding to GQDs and DOX without using external fluorescent dyes. DOX exerts an anti-cancer effect by inhibiting DNA; therefore, uptake/release analysis via fluorescence signal tracking must visualize the nuclear compartment. Free DOX internalization within U251 glioma cells is shown in Fig. S5. To confirm the nuclear localization of the released DOX, the nuclei were stained with a fluorescent nuclear marker (DAPI) and the images were overlapped with the DOX fluorescence images. As illustrated in Fig. S5, DOX molecules gradually diffuse into nuclei with the incubation time, consistent with the results in the literature . Most free DOX reached the nuclei after 8h of incubation. The U251 human glioma cells are incubated with DOX-GQDs-RGD for different periods of time at 37°C. The GQDs and the released DOX can be localized by examination with CLSM, because the GQDs and DOX emit green and red fluorescence, respectively. It is feasible to record the dual-colored fluorescence images, leading to simultaneous observation of the GQDs and DOX localization, thus favoring inspection of both drug delivery and release processes. Fig. 5 reveals that DOX-GQDs-RGD is effectively taken up by U251 glioma cells. During the 16h monitoring period, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs and DOX increased with incubation time. It is obvious that the fluorescence intensity of the nanoassembly at the end of the monitoring period is much higher than that at 2h. This result suggests that some DOX molecules are released, which leads to the partially recovered fluorescence. Similar fluorescence changes were also observed when DOX was released from GQDs–FA [12]. After 2h incubation of U251 glioma cells with DOX-GQDs-RGD (Fig. 5e), the overlap of the green fluorescence from GQDs, red fluorescence from DOX and the blue fluorescence from DAPI illustrate that the DOX-GQDs-RGD mainly accumulate outside cell nuclei. The conjugated DOX of DOX-GQDs-RGD after 2h of incubation is mainly outside cell nuclei and colocalized with GQDs (Fig. 5c). After 8h incubation of U251 glioma cells with DOX-GQDs-RGD (Fig. 5c), the loaded DOX produces more obvious fluorescence in both the nuclei and cytoplasm, demonstrating that a portion of the DOX has been released from the DOX-GQDs-RGD. In comparison with nuclear staining by DAPI, it is also apparent that some GQD nanocarrier started penetrating the cell nuclei, although most of them remain outside the nucleus. With the incubation time increasing from 8 to 16 h, the loaded DOX is mostly found within the cell nuclei. This visual interpretation indicates that DOX release from DOX-GQDs-RGD is sustainable, and DOX might be released to the cytoplasm and eventually enter the cell nuclei, where DOX is known to interact with topoisomerase II to cause DNA cleavage and cytotoxicity [9]. On the other hand, most GQDs reside outside the nucleus as displayed by the overlap of green fluorescence signal (GQDs) and red fluorescence signal (DOX). However, more GQDs have penetrated into the cell nuclei after 16h of incubation, as illustrated in Fig. 5e. The maximum DOX release rate from DOX-GQDs-RGD is 40.1% Therefore, the effective DOX (which must penetrate the nucleus in order to exert its effects) is not entirely DOX released from the DOX-GQDs-RGD assembly; the other part is the DOX-GQDs-RGD conjugate.

In the current work, we proposed a multifunctional nanocarrier consisting of GQDs, RGD, and DOX, used as fluorescent delivery vehicles, a targeting ligand, and a chemotherapeutic drug, respectively. We first functionalized GQDs by covalently conjugating RGD onto the surface of GQDs; subsequently, DOX was loaded onto the surfaces of GQDs-RGD, through π-π stacking and hydrophobic interactions, to produce the target nanoscale drug delivery system, DOX-GQDs-RGD. The loading efficiency of DOX on GQDs-RGD is about 54.6%. The total amount of DOX released was approximately 40.1% under mild acidic conditions (pH 5.0), whereas only 9.2% of DOX was released at near-neutral pH (7.4) after 72h. This study demonstrates that DOX-GQDs-RGD could be a potent killer of cancer cells in vitro. Cell viability assays confirmed that DOX-GQDs-RGD generate a greater cytotoxicity for U251 human glioma cells compared to free DOX. Furthermore, intracellular imaging indicated that DOX-GQDs-RGD could be efficiently taken up by the U251 glioma cells. Because the GQDs and DOX emit green and red fluorescence, respectively, CLSM analysis offers advantages for tracking and localization of both the GQDs and the released DOX, This optical approach, therefore, facilitates inspection of the drug delivery and drug release processes.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51472174). L. F. Dong also thanks the Malmstrom Endowed Fund at Hamline University for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.09.200.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Jian Dong received her B.S. degree in 1995 from Qufu Normal University, and a Ph.D. degree in 2004 from Shandong University. Since then, she has been teaching at Taishan Medical University. She was an associate professor from 2005 to 2010 and became professor in 2011. Her main scientific interest concerns the synthesis of the fluorescence materials for sensing, bioimaging, and drug delivery.

Kaiqi Wang received her B.S. in pharmacy from Taishan Medical University in 2014. At present, she is a graduate student in pharmaceutical engineering at Taishan Medical University. Her current research interest involves the synthesis of the fluorescence materials for bioimaging and drug delivery.

Liping Sun received her B.S. degree in 2000, M.S degree in 2004, and Ph.D. degree in 2012 from Shandong Agricultural University. Since 2004, she has been teaching in Taishan Medical University. Her current research interests involve the biological evaluation and bioimaging of fluorescence materials.

Baoliang Sun received his B.S. degree in 1985 from Weifang Medical University, M.S degree in 1997 from Qingdao University, and Ph.D. degree in 2004 from Huazhong University of Science and Technology. He has been a full Professor and director of the Department of Neurology at Affiliated Hospital of Taishan Medical University since 2001. He had been the vice president of the hospital from 2003 to 2007. He is now the specially appointed Taishan Scholar Professor of Neurology by the government of Shandong Province of China, the director of Key Laboratory of Cerebral Microcirculation at the Universities of Shandong, director of the Institute of Neurological Disorders of Taishan Medical University, and the director of Graduate Student Education of Neurology and Clinical Medicine. He worked as a visiting professor in the San Francisco General Hospital of the University of California, San Francisco (UCSF), USA, in 2009. His current research interests involve cerebrovascular disorders, tumor, and other cellular and molecular studies on different cell and animal models.

Mingfeng Yang received his B.S. degree in 2005 from the Taishan Medical University. He had been an experimentalist at Key Laboratory of Cerebral Microcirculation in the Universities of Shandong, Taishan Medical University. He has been a high-ranking experimentalist from 2016. His current research interests involve cerebrovascular disorders, tumor, and other cellular and molecular studies on different cell and animal models.

Hongyu Chen received his Ph.D. in materials chemistry from Shandong University in 2006 and since then, he has been teaching at Taishan Medical University. He became an associate professor in 2007 and his main scientific interests are the synthesis of the fluorescence materials.

Yi Wang received his M.S. and Ph.D. from Jonbuk University in 2016 and since then, he has been teaching at Taishan Medical University. He became an associate professor in 2016. His current research interests involve tumor research, cellular and molecular tracing studies in cell lines and animal models. Dr. Wang is a member of the Specialized Committee of Ocular Microcirculation in the Chinese Microcirculation Society, the Ophthalmology Ultrasound Specialized Committee in the Chinese Society of Ultrasonic Medical Engineering, the International Society for Neurological Rehabilitation, the Specialized Committee of Hydrogen Molecular Biomedical in China International Exchange, and Promotive Association for Medical and Health Care, China Optometry and Visual Science Society.

Jingyi Sun received his B.S. degree in 2015 from Taishan Medical University. He was a M.S student in 2015 at Qinghai University. He has studied for the Ph.D. degree at Wonju Severance Christian Hospital, Yonsei University Wonju College of Medicine, Republic of Korea, from 2016. His current research interests involve tumor research, and cellular and molecular tracing studies in cell lines and animal models.

Lifeng Dong is the Emma K. and Carl R. N. Malmstrom Endowed Chair in Physics at Hamline University and an adjunct professor of Taishan Medical University. Professor Dong received his Ph.D. and M.S. in physics from Portland State University after completing his M.S. in materials science and engineering and B.S. in mechanical engineering at Qingdao University of Science and Technology in China. Professor Dong currently works with his collaborators and students to design, synthesize/fabricate, and characterize nanoscale materials and devices (including solar cells, fuel cells, supercapacitors, lithium batteries, field effect transistors, and biosensors) for energy conversion and storage as well as water purification and desalination.
